coredata.dc:description: To avoid the excessive corrosion of copper in chemical mechanical polishing, adding corrosion inhibitors to the polishing slurry is one of the effective solutions. Triazoles and their derivatives show great potential for copper protection in various fields because of their chemical activities and limited toxicity. Here, two derivatives of 1, 2, 4-triazole, 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole, and 3,5-diamino-1,2,4-triazole as potential inhibitors for copper corrosion in an alkaline environment (pH = 9) were investigated using experimental techniques and theoretical calculations. The effects of differences in molecular structure and how they affect the interaction mechanism between the inhibitor and metal surface were analyzed. The electrochemical experiments and surface morphology of copper surface analysis proved that the studied three inhibitors acted as anodic-type inhibitors that could effectively inhibit copper corrosion and improve the surface morphology of copper. The adsorption process of three inhibitor molecules obeys the Langmuir monolayer isotherm model of copper. In addition, several adsorption properties such as electronic properties, adsorption geometries and energies, charge transfer, and kinetic process in solvent conditions were discussed using density functional theory and dynamics simulation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To avoid the excessive corrosion of copper in chemical mechanical polishing, adding corrosion inhibitors to the polishing slurry is one of the effective solutions. Triazoles and their derivatives show great potential for copper protection in various fields because of their chemical activities and limited toxicity. Here, two derivatives of 1, 2, 4-triazole, 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole, and 3,5-diamino-1,2,4-triazole as potential inhibitors for copper corrosion in an alkaline environment (pH = 9) were investigated using experimental techniques and theoretical calculations. The effects of differences in molecular structure and how they affect the interaction mechanism between the inhibitor and metal surface were analyzed. The electrochemical experiments and surface morphology of copper surface analysis proved that the studied three inhibitors acted as anodic-type inhibitors that could effectively inhibit copper corrosion and improve the surface morphology of copper. The adsorption process of three inhibitor molecules obeys the Langmuir monolayer isotherm model of copper. In addition, several adsorption properties such as electronic properties, adsorption geometries and energies, charge transfer, and kinetic process in solvent conditions were discussed using density functional theory and dynamics simulation.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper (Cu), the most desirable metal material for multilayer interconnect, is widely applied in integrated circuits industries owing to its physical properties such as excellent electrical conductivity, good resistance to electromigration and distinctive mechanical workability [1]. To achieve flatness and normalization of interconnection structures, the deposited Cu film with an irregular structure profile using the damascene process needs to be flattened by chemical mechanical polishing (CMP) method [2,3]. The Cu CMP process is combined with chemical reaction and mechanical grinding to obtain high selectivity for the removal rate of the convex and concave area [4].

It is well known that copper CMP slurry contains many components, among which complexing agent is the main chemical reagent. Glycine is a commonly used complexing agent for copper CMP, and its complexing reaction with copper ions can effectively accelerate the corrosion of copper and improve the removal rate of copper. Alkaline polishing slurry containing glycine (pH = 9) is commonly used in copper CMP [5,6]. However, copper can be easily corroded by the exposure to alkaline medium containing glycine. The simple and practical method to protect copper in the concave area from corrosion is to introduce organic corrosion inhibitors containing heteroatoms (O, N, S) and electron-rich groups such as (CN, NN, CO, OH, and NH2) into the polishing slurry [7-10].

These organic compounds can effectively be adsorbed on the metal surface and form stable organic protection films on the metal/solution interface to prevent reactive ions from contacting the metal surface and effectively reduce metal dissolution [11-13]. The structural features of the inhibitor, the nature of the metal surface, the composition of the corrosive medium, and the environmental properties are decisive factors for the adsorption of organic compounds on the metal surface [14,15]. In recent years, many researchers in the field of corrosion inhibitors have focused on the correlation between the molecular properties of corrosion inhibitors and their effectiveness [16-19]. Previous studies have revealed that corrosion inhibitors containing functional groups such as the amino group [18], mercapto group [20,21], and benzene group [22] etc, have stronger adsorption performance and higher inhibition efficiency on the copper surface.

Several critical parameters of compounds to be acted as inhibitors for metal corrosion are summarized in Fig. 1 . In recent years, benzotriazole (BTA) and its derivatives have been restricted because they do not meet the requirements of environmentally friendly and biodegradable additives, despite their considerable corrosion inhibition efficiency and low cost [23]. Thus, it is of great significance to design and find environmental compatibility and highly effective corrosion inhibitors. 1, 2, 4-triazole (TAZ) has been regarded as a suitable candidate for substituting conventional toxic inhibitors due to its low toxicity and environmental friendliness [24]. However, compared with BTA, the inhibition efficiency of TAZ is very low. Therefore, it is very necessary to explore the correlation between chemical structure and performance of inhibitors to improve the inhibition performance of TAZ by modifying its molecular structure. It has been reported that the introduction of the amino group (NH2) into TAZ to obtain 3-amino-1,2,4-triazole (ATA) improves the inhibition properties of copper [25]. ATA introduces an amino group to obtain 3,5-diamino-1,2,4-triazole (DAT), which can be used as a chemical intermediate of affinity reagent and synthetic intermediate. However, only a few studies have discussed DAT acting as an inhibitor for copper under an alkaline environment [26-28].

In this paper, 10 mM glycine solution with pH value of 9 is used as reference, two derivatives of TAZ, ATA, and DAT were selected. Based on their molecular structures, the effects of difference in molecular structure on the inhibition performance of copper were investigated using electrochemical measurements combined with surface tests. Meanwhile, the adsorption geometries and energies, electronic properties, and the interfacial adsorption behavior between the inhibitor and Cu-atoms surface were further discussed in a molecular scale by density functional theory (DFT) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. Our research may provide a preliminary understanding of the effects of differences in molecular structure on their adsorption, and may offer guidance for the design and development of novel organic inhibitor molecules in CMP process.

Before the electrochemical measurements, the copper electrode was abraded continuously with 400–2000 girt SiC sandpaper until the entire copper surface was smooth and flat, then immersed in 25 mM citric acid solution for 3 min, washed ultrasonically with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried by nitrogen.

The molecular structures of the three inhibitors,TAZ, ATA, and DAT, are shown in Fig. 2 , which were purchased from Chemart (Tianjin, China) Co., Ltd. with a purity of 99.99% and no purification before use. Three inhibitors of different concentrations (5, 10, 15, and 20 mM) were added to a 10 mM glycine solution, and then the pH value was regulated to 9.0 by KOH and diluted HNO3 for the copper corrosion experiment. The solution without inhibitors was considered as a blank sample for comparison.

All electrochemical measurements were measured using a conventional three-electrode system, in which the copper electrode, exposed area of 1 × 1 cm2, was the working electrode, the platinum (Pt) electrode with a size of 2 × 2 cm2 was the counter electrode, while the saturated calomel electrode was the reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was continuously tested in solution for 20 min to reach an almost steady open circuit potential (E OCP). Secondly, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results were acquired by imposing a 5 mV sinusoidal voltage in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. ZSimDemo software was used for fitting EIS data. Finally, the potentiodynamic polarization curves were conducted in the potential range of E OCP ± 300 mV at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. All experiments were repeated three times to ensure reliability.

The surface characteristic (morphology and roughness) of copper coupons (1 × 1 cm2) with different solutions treatment was measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 500, ZEISS, Germany) and atomic force microscope (AFM, 5600LS, Agilent), before and after immersion in 10 mM glycine solution (pH = 9.0), without and with 20 mM TAZ, 20 mM ATA, and 15 mM DAT, respectively. Before all morphology tests, the copper coupons were washed by DI water and dried by nitrogen.

The three inhibitor molecules were calculated by Material Studio (MS, version 2019) software via the DMol3 module. Quantum chemical descriptors were calculated applying DFT method with generalized gradient approximation (GGA) - Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange–correlation functional and double numeric plus polarization (DNP) basis set. Firstly, the molecular structures of TAZ, ATA, and DAT were optimized via vibrational analysis to ensure that the tested molecules reached the minimum point of potential energy[29]. Then the corresponding quantum chemical descriptors containing orbital energies (E HOMO, E LUMO, and E Gap), Fukui function, and electrostatic potential (ESP) were calculated and performed.

The adsorption configuration and corresponding energy of inhibitor on the Cu-atoms surface were performed with the CASTEP module in MS computing package. Firstly, a 4 × 4 Cu (111) supercell model with four atomic layers was taken as the adsorption surface of the inhibitor molecule, where the bottom two layers were fixed in all of the geometry optimization calculations. A 15 Å vacuum layer was constructed to eliminate the interaction between the adsorbate and the Cu- atoms layers [30]. The GGA of PBE for exchange–correlation functional and ultrasoft pseudo-potential were used. The parameter criteria for energy, Maximum force, and Maximum displacement were 1 × 10−5 eV/atom, 3 × 10−2 eV/Å, and 1 × 10−3 Å, respectively [31]. The k-points was set as 3 × 3 × 1, and the maximum cutoff energy was 400 eV. The test results for energy and convergence show the reliability of the parameter setting. All calculations in this part were performed in the exact same conditions, so we can compare the adsorption energy change with each other to evaluate the site preference and the stability of the adsorption configuration, which is calculated by the following formula [32]:
(1)

where E ads, E tot, E mol, and E sub denote the adsorption energy, the total energy of the system, the energy of the inhibitor molecule, and the energy of the substrate, respectively.

The kinetic process and adsorption orientation of inhibitor on the surface of Cu (111) in a solvent condition were studied by MD simulation with the Forcite module in MS. Firstly, a 6 × 6 Cu (111) supercell with six atomic layers was created as a substrate. A single inhibitor molecule and 500 water molecules were randomly filled into a water box, which served as an intermediate layer to bring the simulation closer to the actual solution environment [33]. A vacuum layer of 15 Å was placed between the substrate and the intermediate layer to avoid interaction between the two. All simulations were carried out using COMPASSII force field and NVT constant canonical ensemble at 298 K. Ewald and Atom base methods were selected to calculate the electrostatic and van der Waals interactions, respectively [34]. The whole simulation time was set to 1000 ps and a time step was 1 ps to ensure the inhibitor could be fully adsorbed on the copper substrate. Furthermore, the interaction energy or binding energy between the researched inhibitor and copper substrate can be defined as follows [35]:

where E int, E bin, and E sub+H2O refer to the interaction energy, the binding energy, and the energy of the substrate and H2O, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The evaluation of the inhibition performance

ce:section.ce:para: The OCP curves of the copper electrode in the solutions composed of 10 mM glycine and different concentrations of TAZ, ATA, and DAT are presented in Fig. 3 . It is found that the E OCP fluctuation range of all OCP curves under different conditions is less than 3 mV at the last 300 s (test time 900 ∼ 1200 s), which suggests that the adsorption and desorption behavior of inhibitor on the copper electrode surface have reached a dynamic balance when the OCP test lasted for 1200 s [11,36]. After adding three inhibitors, the OCP of the copper electrode obviously moves in the positive direction, which indicates that high-resistance substances on the copper electrode surface are formed.

Tafel polarization curves of copper electrode treated with 10 mM glycine solution and different TAZ, ATA, and DAT concentrations are displayed in Fig. 4 . It is noteworthy that all of the cathodic and anodic branches of Tafel curves tend to shift towards a low current density direction with the introduction of three inhibitors and the increase of concentration. However, the slope of the cathodic polarization curves of three inhibitors does not change significantly with respect to the blank solution, manifesting that the cathodic reaction process (dissolved oxygen reaction) basically remains unchanged in the presence of three inhibitors [37]. Compared with the anodic branch of blank, the slope of the anodic branch in the Tafel polarization region increases with the introduction of three inhibitors, suggesting that these three inhibitors mainly limit the dissolution of copper during the anodic reaction [6,19].

The electrochemical corrosion parameters, including corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), cathodic Tafel slope (β c), and anodic Tafel slope (β a), were extracted from the Tafel curves and were listed in Table 1 . The inhibition efficiency (η T) can be defined as follows [38]:
(3)
η T
( % )
=
i corr,0
-
i corr
i corr,0
× 100
in which, i corr and i corr,0 are the current density with and without inhibitors, respectively.

It can be noticed from Table 1 that with the addition of three inhibitors, the maximum deviation of corrosion potential exceeds 85 mV, so these three inhibitors belong to anodic inhibitors for copper [39,40]. It is further seen that with the introduction of three inhibitors, the value of E corr shows an increasing trend, but the value of i corr decreases markedly, which also proves that the inhibitor adsorption film with high impedance is formed to the surface so as to effectually prevent the further oxidation of copper and/or the corrosion of copper surfaces by dissolved oxygen [41]. The maximum inhibition efficiencies of these inhibitors are 92.2%, 92.5%, and 96.4% at concentrations of 20 mM, 20 mM, and 15 mM, respectively. This also indicates that the three inhibitors show good inhibition properties for copper corrosion in an alkaline environment containing glycine, and the inhibition efficiency follows the order: DAT > ATA > TAZ. By observing the molecular structure of TAZ, ATA, and DAT, as shown in Fig. 1, it can be deduced that introducing amino groups into the TAZ molecule is beneficial for enhancing its inhibition performance.

Fig. 5 shows the Nyquist and Bode plots recorded of copper electrodes in the solutions composed of 10 mM glycine with various concentrations of TAZ, ATA, and DAT, respectively. Obviously, a suppressed capacitive loop can be seen in Fig. 5(a)-(c), which corresponds to the charge-transfer reactions controlled by double-layer capacitance and the charge transfer resistance, which can be regarded as a criterion to evaluate the inhibition performance [42]. Moreover, the main shape of the capacitive loop does not change, which suggests that the reaction mechanism of the copper surface is not affected by three different inhibitors [43,44]. Nevertheless, it can be found that these semicircle centers of impedance spectra are below the horizontal axis, that is, frequency dispersion effect, suggesting the non-ideal capacitive behavior at the copper/solution interface. This is ascribed to the roughness of the metal surface and the chemical heterogeneity of inhibitor molecules [45]. The size of impedance plots in the addition of three inhibitors is greater than that obtained in the blank solution, indicating that the inhibitor-adsorption films on the copper surface are formed. Moreover, the capacitive loop increases with the increase of TAZ or ATA concentration, suggesting that more inhibitor molecules get adsorbed on the surface of copper, and at the same time, the inhibitor-adsorption films become more and more complete, and finally improve the inhibition performance. Particularly, when the concentration of DAT increases from 0 to 20 mM, the diameter of capacitive loop gradually increases, and then decreases, reaching the maximum value at 15 mM. This phenomenon may be explained that with the further increase of concentration of DAT, the interaction of the inhibitor molecules with the copper surface gradually increases, resulting in the desorption of the inhibitor or the change of the adsorption morphology, which leads to the reduction of the inhibition efficiency of the inhibitor [16]. As can be found from Fig. 5(d)-(f), the value of impedance modulus at a lower frequency (Zf = 0.1 Hz) is continuously increased by the introduction of three inhibitors, and the increase of concentration and its highest value is approximately an order of magnitude higher than that of the blank solution. At the same time, the value of the largest phase angle in medium and low frequency also augments with an increase in inhibitor concentration, which may be due to the formation of inhibitor-adsorption films on the surface of copper [46].

Two equivalent circuits depicted in Fig. 6 were selected to analyze and fit the results of impedance spectra. Table 2 shows the inhibitor-adsorption film resistance (R f), charge transfer resistance (R ct), polarization resistance (R p = R f + R ct), Warburg impedance (W), and constant phase elements (CPE f and CPE dl), which are used to replace film capacitance (C f) and double-layer capacitance (C dl) due to the non-ideal capacitive behavior of copper electrode surface. The CPE impedance and capacitance values can be estimated using the equation [47,48]:
(4)
Z CPE

in which, Y0 refers to the CPE constant, j and ω stand for the imaginary root and the angular frequency, and n (varying from −1 to 1) is the deviation parameter. When the value of n is −1, 0, 0.5, and 1, the ZCPE corresponds to the inductor, resistor, Warburg impedance, and capacitor, respectively.

Clearly, as shown in Table 2, with the introduction of three inhibitors and the concentration augments, the values of R ct and R p increase significantly, indicating that the adsorption of three inhibitors markedly inhibits the corrosion reaction on the copper electrode. Thus the process of charge transfer can be suppressed [13,49]. However, when the concentration of DAT is greater than15mM, R ct and R p decrease, indicating that there is a desorption process on the electrode surface. Furthermore, the values of C dl and C f change irregularly, but all of the capacitance values significantly decrease relative to that of the blank solution. It can be inferred that the inhibitors replace the pre-adsorbed water to form inhibitor-adsorption films on the copper surface. The inhibition efficiency (η E) values, determined by the equation (6), show an increasing trend with the concentration of inhibitors and reach the maximum values of 91.6%, 92.3%, and 96.5% at 20, 20, and 15 mM, respectively. The inhibition efficiency sequence of the three inhibitors is consistent with the previous conclusions for polarization curves and reveals that the introduction of amino groups is beneficial for improving the inhibition efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies of copper coupons were evaluated through SEM and AFM measurements after being immersed in 10 mM glycine, 10 mM glycine and 20 mM TAZ, 10 mM glycine and 20 mM ATA, and 10 mM glycine and 15 mM TAZ for 5 min, and the corresponding images are shown in Fig. 7 . It can be observed from Fig. 7(a) and (e) that the copper surface after immersion in blank glycine solution without inhibitor was severely damaged and appeared to have evident corrosion. Fig. 7(b-d) and 7(f-h) display that the copper surfaces are relatively smoother with only some small pitting in the addition of three inhibitors. By comparing the images of copper surfaces captured in the blank glycine solution, it is clear to judge that the copper corrosion was considerably inhibited, which confirms that these inhibitors get effectively adsorbed on the copper surface. Correspondingly, the root mean square surface roughness (Sq) values for the blank and the inhibited (TAZ, ATA, and DAT) samples are 3.70 nm, 1.54 nm, 1.04 nm, and 0.88 nm, respectively. From the above images and values of Sq, it can be confirmed that their sequence of inhibition ability is DAT > ATA > TAZ, which is highly compatible with previous electrochemical results.

Based on the above, the inhibition efficiency and anti-corrosive performance of the three inhibitor molecules are characterized by electrochemical experiments and surface tests. To determine the absorption type of three inhibitors on the copper surface, we used the EIS data to fit various adsorption isotherm models. The results suggest that the adsorption process of these inhibitors obeys the Langmuir monolayer adsorption, as all linear regression coefficients (R 2) are close to 1. The Langmuir isotherm equation is shown below [50,51]:
(7)
C inh
θ
=
1
K ads
+
C inh

in which, C inh refers to the concentration of inhibitor, θ, described as η E/100, stands for the surface coverage, and K ads is the equilibrium constant. The standard adsorption free energy ΔG ads can be estimated by the values of K ads using the following equation [52,53]:
(8)
K

)
in which, R and T refer to the molar gas constant and absolute temperature, respectively.

The fitting curves of C inh/θ as a function of C inh with an intercept of 1/K ads and the calculated thermodynamic parameters, including K ads and ΔG ads of three inhibitors, are shown in Fig. 8 . As is known to all, the larger the value of K ads, the smaller the value of ΔG ads, indicating that the adsorption of organic compounds on the metal surface is stronger, and the inhibition performance is better [54]. The order of K ads of the three inhibitors is DAT > ATA > TAZ, while the order of ΔG ads values is opposite. The above results manifest that DAT molecules possess powerful adsorption capacity on the copper surface and show higher inhibition efficiency than ATA and TAZ, which is consistent with their inhibition efficiency obtained from electrochemical experiments. Moreover, the value of ΔG ads greater than −20 kJ/mol means physisorption, which is due to the electrostatic attraction between the charged metal and inhibitor molecules; while the value of ΔG ads lower than −40 kJ/mol represents chemisorption, which is caused by the charge transfer and/or sharing from inhibitor molecules to metal [36,55]. The calculated ΔG ads values of TAZ, ATA, and DAT are −25.6, −28.9, and −35.5 kJ/mol, respectively. The result confirms that their adsorption types belong to mixed adsorption, including physisorption and chemisorption. Besides, the values of ΔG ads of the three inhibitors are all negative, which reveals that the adsorption process of TAZ, ATA, and DAT is spontaneous [34].

The adsorption analysis should first need to consider the possible reaction sites of the inhibitor molecule. The optimized structures of three inhibitor molecules and their charge density distribution of frontier orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) as well as ESP are displayed in Fig. 9 . These relevant quantum chemical descriptors, including E HOMO, E LUMO, and ΔE (E LUMO- E HOMO), can be seen in Table S1.

Obviously, these inhibitor molecules show planar conjugated structures, which means that they can be absorbed parallel onto the copper surface [13]. The frontier orbitals of electron cloud density are evenly distributed throughout the five-membered ring of the TAZ molecule. When the amino-functional groups are added to the TAZ molecules, the electron clouds on HOMO and LUMO orbitals are obviously transferred from the nitrogen-containing five-membered rings to nitrogen-containing chains, which indicates that the introduced amino groups can act as reactive groups to bond with the copper surface through electron transfer [56].

As expected, it is observed that the electron-rich regions are mainly concentrated around the amino groups from the ESP. By comparing the three molecules, DAT has more electron-rich regions than ATA and TAZ, which indicates that DAT molecules are easier to form coordinated covalent bonds with copper atoms by transferring or sharing free electrons from inhibitor molecules to the electrophile (copper).

It is well known that the lower E LUMO corresponds to the better electron-accepting ability of organic molecules. At the same time, the higher E HOMO indicates the better electron-donating ability of the molecules to the empty orbitals of copper [57]. Besides, the energy gap value (E Gap = E LUMO − E HOMO) is also an important theoretical descriptor to estimate the adsorption ability of corrosion inhibitors and inhibition performance[58]. As is evident from Table S1, the calculated E Gap values suggest the following trend of inhibition efficiency: DAT > ATA > TAZ, which indicates that DAT and ATA act as better inhibitors than TAZ [51].

Fukui function was used to analyze the nucleophilic or electrophilic reactivity sites within the inhibitor molecules, which can be expressed as the following equation [59]:
(9)
fi
(
r →
)
+
= q i ( N +1) - q i
N
(10)
fi
(
r →
)
-
= q i ( N ) - q i
( N - 1 )

in which, the parameters qi (N + 1), qi (N), and qi (N-1) stand for the atomic charge of cationic, neutral, and anionic species, respectively. The nucleophilic and electrophilic attack indexes are defined by fi + and fi - . The larger value of fi + means that the atoms have a greater probability of accepting electrons during inhibitor-copper interactions. Moreover, a larger value of fi - is inferred to have more electrophilic reactive sites within the inhibitor molecules, implying the atom's high tendency to donate electrons [41]. Table S2 shows Fukui function of three inhibitor molecules, and it can be found that there are more atoms with comparatively larger Fukui function values in the DAT (N2, N5, N7, and N10) and ATA (N2, N5, and N8) molecule than TAZ (N2 and N5), which is attributed to the introduction of amino groups. In addition, these heteroatoms are preferentially considered in the interactions of the inhibitor molecules with the surface Cu-atoms.

We consider two conformations of inhibitor molecule absorbed on the Cu-atoms surface, as given in Fig. 10 . One was parallel adsorption, and the other was vertical one by N atoms with the surface Cu atoms. The parallel configurations of three inhibitor molecules in Fig. 10(a-c) show relatively weak adsorption with adsorption energies of −0.03 eV, −0.06 eV, and −0.22 eV, respectively. In this context, the distance of the inhibitor - Cu surface is greater than 3 Å, within the van der Waals radius (3 Å – 5 Å), which can be categorized as weak physical adsorption [60,61]. As shown in Fig. 10(d), TAZ is vertically adsorbed on the Cu-atoms surface with an adsorption energy of −0.45 eV. The single N5 atom of TAZ is directly coordinated with surface Cu atoms with a bond length of 2.04 Å. In Fig. 10(e), the N2 atom of ATA bonds to surface Cu with a bond length of 2.05 Å, and the H atoms in its amino groups interact with Cu-atoms to form hydrogen bonds as well. Fig. 10(f) exhibits the N5 and N7 atoms of DAT molecule form covalent bonds with surface Cu-atoms with bond lengths of 2.09 and 2.18 Å, respectively. Similarly, a CuH bond is formed during the DAT adsorption. The formed Cu-N adsorption bond lengths are close to the sum of their respective covalent radii (
r
cov
Cu
+
r
cov
N
=1.32 + 0.71 = 2.03 Å), indicating strong chemical adsorption occurring in vertical configurations [28]. As far as the obtained adsorption energies are concerned, their adsorption strength increases in the following order: TAZ < ATA < DAT, which is highly compatible with the experimentally observed inhibition efficiency sequence. At the same time, we can also find that the vertical conformation is more stable than the parallel one.

The adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface is characterized by chemical bonds, while the form of chemical bonds often leads to the deformation of charge density. Fig. 11 displays the two-dimensional slice results of the charge density difference of the most stable configuration. This distinctly demonstrates charge distribution between the interacting atoms and the Cu-atoms surface. The bright blue regions represent charge accumulation, while the red ones indicate charge depletion. It can be seen that the charge accumulation mainly appears at the interface between the adsorbed inhibitor and Cu surface, which suggests the formation of direct Cu–N covalent bonds. A relatively larger charge accumulation area in DAT adsorption configuration than ATA and TAZ confirms that the introduction of amino groups significantly promotes the adsorption performance. In addition, there exist a red area under the H atom in the amino group, which is considered to be the presence of weak CuH bonds.

In order to quantitatively analyze the charge redistribution between the inhibitors and Cu surface, we next output the charge density difference curve along the Z-axis, as shown in Fig. 12 . In the curves, the blue-filled curve (right) represents the charge accumulation, and the yellow-filled one means the charge diffusion. It can be observed that the charge redistribution between the inhibitors and the Cu surface implying that the adsorbed inhibitors act as charge acceptors to extract electrons from the Cu-atoms surface. As is known to all, a larger magnitude of electron accumulation between adsorbate and substrate indicates stronger chemisorption and correlates closely with adsorption energy [62,63]. As demonstrated in Fig. 12, a higher magnitude of charge accumulation area (around 6 Å−8 Å) was found in the DAT adsorption with two CuN bond configurations, suggesting its stronger interaction with the Cu-atoms surface. This further proves the beneficial effect of introducing amino groups in improving the adsorption performance.

Considering that the actual interaction of organic inhibitors on the metal surface occurs in an aqueous environment, the MD simulation was applied to study and clarify the adsorption characteristics of inhibitors on the Cu surface in the aqueous phase and provided a more detailed study on the adsorption mechanism under actual applications [64,65]. The corresponding equilibrium adsorption configurations obtained from MD simulation are presented in Fig. 13 . The figure clearly shows that three inhibitor molecules tend to be adsorbed on the Cu surface in a parallel way and in close contact with the Cu surface. This adsorption configuration can provide maximum coverage to minimize the corrosion of copper by corrosive media [66]. The obtained E bin values are 1.58 eV for TAZ, 1.95 eV for ATA, and 2.15 eV for DAT. The higher energy value indicates the better and more stable adsorption of inhibitor molecules. Thus, DAT exhibits the strongest adsorption performance, followed by ATA and TAZ, which conforms with the above analysis.

In order to further illustrate the characteristics of chemical bonds formed by the molecule-surface interaction, the radial distribution function (RDF) of the three inhibitors was calculated by MD simulation [67]. It is generally believed that the first prominent peak position at less than 3.5 Å is associated with chemical adsorption. In contrast, physical interaction is associated with the peaks at a position greater than 3.5 Å [68,69]. Fig. 14 shows RDF calculation results of reactive N-atoms in three inhibitor molecules, such as nitrogen atoms in the five-membered rings (N2 and N5) and the nitrogen atoms in amino groups (N7). It is worth highlighting that the appearance of the first peak in all Cu-N bonds is less than 3.5 Å, indicating that the investigated N-atoms are close to the Cu surface, and there are chemical interactions between them. The RDF results also support the conjecture of Section 3.5.

The inhibition effects of two derivatives of TAZ containing one or two amino groups (ATA, DAT) on copper in an alkaline environment (pH = 9) were studied at various concentrations. By means of the electrochemical experiment evaluations, we found that the three inhibitors acted as anodic-type inhibitors that could effectively inhibit copper corrosion in an alkaline environment. The inhibition efficiency follows the order: DAT > ATA > TAZ, which is confirmed by SEM and AFM morphology of copper surface analysis. In terms of the results of fitting Langmuir adsorption isotherm, DAT and ATA have larger adsorbed equilibrium constants and smaller standard adsorption free energy than TAZ, implying that they have better physisorption and chemisorption capacity on the copper surface. Also, the adsorption mechanism of three inhibitors on the Cu surface was investigated by DFT and MD simulation. As far as the studies of molecular activity analysis are concerned, the amino groups in DAT and ATA increase the active centers of the inhibitor molecules and enhance their ability to form chemical coordination with copper atoms. Two adsorption conformations of inhibitor molecule on Cu-atoms surface were further established, and the corresponding adsorption energy had a good correlation with their inhibition efficiency obtained from the experiments. Analyses of molecular dynamics simulation show that three studied inhibitors tend to be adsorbed on the Cu surface in a parallel mode. This work demonstrates the promise of DAT and ATA to become potential inhibitors in copper interconnection CMP and guides to design and develop novel organic inhibitor molecules.
